AgentConfig.description: LLM and concurrency knobs for the verifier.

thinking.description: Enable provider extended-thinking / reasoning for verifier runs. `true` uses the provider's default; a string picks an explicit effort level; `false` disables.
thinking.title: Thinking
thinking.markdownDescription: Enable provider extended-thinking / reasoning for verifier runs. `true` uses the provider's default; a string picks an explicit effort level; `false` disables.

AgentConfig.title: AgentConfig
AgentConfig.markdownDescription: LLM and concurrency knobs for the verifier.

ObservabilityConfig.description:
Project-level Logfire / tracing preferences.

Tracing is opt-in via the `LOGFIRE_TOKEN` env var; this section
only controls how spans are produced once tracing is on.

dangerously_disable_log_scrubbing.description: Disable Logfire's default secret-pattern scrubber (which redacts attribute values matching `password`, `token`, `secret`, etc.). For a security-review tool the scrubber is mostly false positives — the agent legitimately reads code containing those words. Set true only when you trust the dashboard the `LOGFIRE_TOKEN` points at; raw span content (including any real secrets the codebase exposes) will reach it.
dangerously_disable_log_scrubbing.title: Dangerously Disable Log Scrubbing
dangerously_disable_log_scrubbing.markdownDescription: Disable Logfire's default secret-pattern scrubber (which redacts attribute values matching `password`, `token`, `secret`, etc.). For a security-review tool the scrubber is mostly false positives — the agent legitimately reads code containing those words. Set true only when you trust the dashboard the `LOGFIRE_TOKEN` points at; raw span content (including any real secrets the codebase exposes) will reach it.

ObservabilityConfig.title: ObservabilityConfig
ObservabilityConfig.markdownDescription:
Project-level Logfire / tracing preferences.

Tracing is opt-in via the `LOGFIRE_TOKEN` env var; this section
only controls how spans are produced once tracing is on.

OutputConfig.description: Output defaults. Individual reporters are listed in `reporters`.
OutputConfig.title: OutputConfig
OutputConfig.markdownDescription: Output defaults. Individual reporters are listed in `reporters`.

ReporterConfig.description: One reporter instance. `output` is required for file-emitting reporters.
ReporterConfig.title: ReporterConfig
ReporterConfig.markdownDescription: One reporter instance. `output` is required for file-emitting reporters.

RequirementSelector.description: Filters applied on top of the requirements pulled from packs.

include.description: Requirement ids or glob patterns to keep.
include.title: Include
include.markdownDescription: Requirement ids or glob patterns to keep.

exclude.description: Requirement ids or glob patterns to drop.
exclude.title: Exclude
exclude.markdownDescription: Requirement ids or glob patterns to drop.

RequirementSelector.title: RequirementSelector
RequirementSelector.markdownDescription: Filters applied on top of the requirements pulled from packs.

ScopeConfig.description:
Which files in the repo are eligible for analysis.

Scope filtering is for **files the verifier shouldn't bother
analyzing** — vendored dependencies, build outputs, tests with
intentional security flaws. It is NOT a place to limit what the
verifier *checks*; for that, use `requirements.exclude`, which
carries an explicit rationale per excluded requirement.

Sensible defaults are applied automatically (gitignore, common
build and cache directories, binary file extensions). Most
projects don't need to set anything here at all.

include.description: Glob patterns to include. Empty (the default) means the whole repo, minus the automatic skips and any patterns in `exclude`. Use this only when you genuinely need to restrict analysis to a subset (e.g. monorepo scoping) — NOT to limit which requirements run. README, package.json, Dockerfile, and similar config/manifest files are often load-bearing for verification; restricting to source directories will hide them from the verifier.
include.title: Include
include.markdownDescription: Glob patterns to include. Empty (the default) means the whole repo, minus the automatic skips and any patterns in `exclude`. Use this only when you genuinely need to restrict analysis to a subset (e.g. monorepo scoping) — NOT to limit which requirements run. README, package.json, Dockerfile, and similar config/manifest files are often load-bearing for verification; restricting to source directories will hide them from the verifier.

exclude.description: Glob patterns to exclude, applied after gitignore and the built-in skips. Common additions: `**/*.test.ts`, `**/*.spec.ts`, `**/__tests__/**`, `**/fixtures/**`, `**/*.min.js`.
exclude.title: Exclude
exclude.markdownDescription: Glob patterns to exclude, applied after gitignore and the built-in skips. Common additions: `**/*.test.ts`, `**/*.spec.ts`, `**/__tests__/**`, `**/fixtures/**`, `**/*.min.js`.

ScopeConfig.title: ScopeConfig
ScopeConfig.markdownDescription:
Which files in the repo are eligible for analysis.

Scope filtering is for **files the verifier shouldn't bother
analyzing** — vendored dependencies, build outputs, tests with
intentional security flaws. It is NOT a place to limit what the
verifier *checks*; for that, use `requirements.exclude`, which
carries an explicit rationale per excluded requirement.

Sensible defaults are applied automatically (gitignore, common
build and cache directories, binary file extensions). Most
projects don't need to set anything here at all.

Suppression.description:
A reviewed, time-bounded acceptance of a known finding.

Scope is set by either `path` (location-based, glob-aware) or
`fingerprint` (content-based, location-independent) — they are
mutually exclusive. With neither, the suppression applies repo-wide
to every finding raised by `requirement`.

path.description: Path or glob the suppression applies to. Omit for repo-wide.
path.title: Path
path.markdownDescription: Path or glob the suppression applies to. Omit for repo-wide.

fingerprint.description: Stable finding fingerprint, for code-location-independent suppression.
fingerprint.title: Fingerprint
fingerprint.markdownDescription: Stable finding fingerprint, for code-location-independent suppression.

reason.description: Human explanation; required so the rationale is auditable.
reason.title: Reason
reason.markdownDescription: Human explanation; required so the rationale is auditable.

expires.description: Date after which the suppression no longer applies.
expires.title: Expires
expires.markdownDescription: Date after which the suppression no longer applies.

Suppression.title: Suppression
Suppression.markdownDescription:
A reviewed, time-bounded acceptance of a known finding.

Scope is set by either `path` (location-based, glob-aware) or
`fingerprint` (content-based, location-independent) — they are
mutually exclusive. With neither, the suppression applies repo-wide
to every finding raised by `requirement`.

version.description: Config schema version. Bumped on breaking changes.
version.title: Version
version.markdownDescription: Config schema version. Bumped on breaking changes.

packs.description: Built-in pack identifiers or paths to custom pack files. Multiple packs are merged; conflicting requirement ids are an error.

anyOf.title: asvs-l1
anyOf.description:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 1: critical starting point covering ~20% of requirements. Focuses on first-layer defenses against common attacks that don't require other vulnerabilities to be exploitable.
anyOf.markdownDescription:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 1: critical starting point covering ~20% of requirements. Focuses on first-layer defenses against common attacks that don't require other vulnerabilities to be exploitable.

anyOf.title: asvs-l2
anyOf.description:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 2: recommended for most applications, covering ~70% of requirements. Adds protections against less common attacks and more complex defenses that may require certain preconditions to exploit.
anyOf.markdownDescription:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 2: recommended for most applications, covering ~70% of requirements. Adds protections against less common attacks and more complex defenses that may require certain preconditions to exploit.

anyOf.title: asvs-l3
anyOf.description:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 3: highest level of security covering all requirements. Adds defense-in-depth mechanisms and hard-to-implement controls for applications demanding the strongest assurance.
anyOf.markdownDescription:
The Application Security Verification Standard is a list of application security requirements that architects, developers, testers, security professionals, tool vendors, and consumers can use to define, build, test, and verify secure applications.

Level 3: highest level of security covering all requirements. Adds defense-in-depth mechanisms and hard-to-implement controls for applications demanding the strongest assurance.

anyOf.title: masvs
anyOf.description: The OWASP MASVS (Mobile Application Security Verification Standard) is the industry standard for mobile app security. It can be used by mobile software architects and developers seeking to develop secure mobile apps, as well as security testers to ensure completeness and consistency of test results.
anyOf.markdownDescription: The OWASP MASVS (Mobile Application Security Verification Standard) is the industry standard for mobile app security. It can be used by mobile software architects and developers seeking to develop secure mobile apps, as well as security testers to ensure completeness and consistency of test results.

anyOf.description: Path to a custom pack YAML file.
anyOf.markdownDescription: Path to a custom pack YAML file.

packs.title: Packs
packs.markdownDescription: Built-in pack identifiers or paths to custom pack files. Multiple packs are merged; conflicting requirement ids are an error.

context.description: Codebase context provided to the verifier as Markdown. Orients the verifier on architecture, tech stack, and scope so it spends fewer tokens re-discovering them from source.
examples: This is a Vue SPA with a FastAPI backend. Auth via Auth0, so password-hashing requirements don't apply.
context.title: Context
context.markdownDescription: Codebase context provided to the verifier as Markdown. Orients the verifier on architecture, tech stack, and scope so it spends fewer tokens re-discovering them from source.
